rulings.Case Title: PROVIDENT CREDIT UNION VS. CHRISTINA JOHNSON et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Vacate Renewal Of Default Judgment
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 21, 2025, Line 1, DEFENDANT CHRISTINA JOHNSON's Motion To Vacate Renewal Of Default Judgment. Moving party Christina Johnson's motion to vacate renewal of default judgment is ordered off calendar as untimely filed and served. A noticed motion must be filed and served at least 16 court days in advance of the hearing (and court days excludes any court holidays). That filing and service period is extended by five calendar days where the motion is served by first-class mail within California, as Johnson served her motion here. (See Code of Civil Procedure 1005(b).) For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(301/CVA).

rulings.Case Title: JERMILA MCCOY VS. AMERICAN HONDA MOTOR CO., INC., A CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Special Interrogatories From Defendant American Honda Motor Co., Inc., And Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 21, 2025, Line 6, PLAINTIFF JERMILA MCCOY's Motion To Compel Further Responses To Special Interrogatories From Defendant American Honda Motor Co., Inc., And Request For Sanctions. Plaintiff Jermilla McCoy seeks an order compelling defendant American Honda Motor Co. Inc.'s responses to her special interrogatories 62-64, which seek information relating to deposition transcripts in Kathleen Cadena, et al., v. American Honda Motor Company, Inc., et al., United States District Court, Central District of California, Case No. 2:18-cv-04007-MWF-PJW. McCoy contends that the Cadena case also concerns the collision mitigation braking system in Hondas of the type she owned. The court grants the motion and orders AHC to provide substantive responses to the interrogatories. McCoy's rationale on reply is persuasive. AHC's knowledge of braking system issues in Hondas produced in 2018 and subsequent years conceivably bears on whether its response to McCoy's complaint of similar issues was a willful violation of its Song-Beverly obligations. McCoy already has (because this court ordered production of) documents that AHC produced in Cadena. Answering questions about the depositions taken in that case is a quick route to understanding the staggering amount of discovery that AHC has produced in this case. The court accordingly concludes under the unusual circumstances presented here that good cause is shown. AHC has not provided a declaration substantiating in burdens objection to answering the interrogatories on the basis of its best available current knowledge. The court denies sanctions, finding that both parties acted with substantial justification. AHC shall provide verified responses within 30 days of entry of this order. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the moving party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(301/CVA).

rulings.Case Title: JERMILA MCCOY VS. AMERICAN HONDA MOTOR CO., INC., A CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses From Defendant, And Request For Sanctions Set Two Of Requests For Production Of Documents
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 21, 2025, Line 6, PLAINTIFF JERMILA MCCOY's Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses From Defendant, And Request For Sanctions Set Two Of Requests For Production Of Documents. Plaintiff Jermilla McCoy seeks an order compelling defendant American Honda Motor Co. Inc.'s responses to her request for production no. 50, which seeks all transcripts in AHM's possession from depositions taken in Kathleen Cadena, et al., v. American Honda Motor Company, Inc., et al., United States District Court, Central District of California Case No. 2:18-cv-04007-MWF-PJW. For the reasons stated in the court's companion order granting McCoy's motion to compel further responses to related special interrogatories, in the unique circumstances of this case, where McCoy has already received voluminous discovery and seeks an expeditious basis to understand the discovery, McCoy has shown good cause to compel the production and AHM has not substantiated its burdens argument. The court grants the motion but denies sanctions, finding that both parties acted with substantial justification. AHC shall provide responsive documents within 30 days of entry of this order. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. rulings.Case Title: IVAN RESENIKOFF VS. FCA US LLC, A DELAWARE LIMITED LIABILITY COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Request For Production From Defendant Fca Us Llc, And Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 21, 2025, Line 7, PLAINTIFF IVAN RESENIKOFF's Motion To Compel Further Responses To Request For Production From Defendant FCA Us LLC, And Request For Sanctions. Plaintiff Ivan Resnikoff's motion to compel further responses to Request for Production Nos. 45 and 46 is denied. Resnikoff sought an order from this court that defendant FCA US LLC must produce documents evidencing complaints by 2023 Jeep Grand Cherokee owners that were similar to complaints made by Resnikoff (RFP 45) and documents evidencing any warranty repairs to any 2023 Jeep Grand Cherokee vehicles (RFP 46). FCA initially objected but after this motion was filed supplemented its answers and represented that it would produce responsive documents (Smith Dec. Ex. 12.) Ordinarily, a responding party's amendment while a discovery motion is pending does not moot the motion. (See Sinaiko Healthcare Consulting, Inc. v. Pacific Healthcare Consultants (2007) 148 Cal.App.4th 390.) Here, however, Resnikoff's RFP 45 was vague (what complaints and how to discern similarity, where the complaint does not say what complaints Resnikoff had about the vehicle?), and his RFP 46 was very broad, and the parties have made more progress through their meet and confer efforts than the court could or would have ordered. Moreover, the reply brief does not indicate what further production if any Resnikoff seeks. Accordingly, in these circumstances no court order is appropriate. The court finds that both parties acted with substantial justification and does not award sanctions. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. rulings.Case Title: ELIZABETH BROOKS VS. DIAMOND WELLNESS INCORPORATED ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Plaintiff Elizabeth Brooks' Verified Responses To Requests For Production Of Documents, Set One, And Request For Sanctions In The Amount Of $1,275.00; Declaration Of Brianda Herrera In Support Thereof
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 21, 2025, Line 8, DEFENDANT DIAMOND WELLNESS INCORPORATED's Motion To Compel Plaintiff Elizabeth Brooks' Verified Responses To Requests For Production Of Documents, Set One, And Request For Sanctions In The Amount Of $1,275.00. Hearing required. The unopposed motion to compel plaintiff Elizabeth Brooks to verify her responses to requests for production of documents, set one, is granted. (See CCP 2031.250(a) ["The party to whom the demand for inspection, copying, testing, or sampling is directed shall sign the response under oath unless the response contains only objections."].) Brooks is ordered to verify her responses within 10 days of entry of this order. Sanctions are appropriate on a discovery motion where the objecting party does not act with substantial justification in its discovery compliance, even where she does not oppose the motion. (See CCP 2031.300(c); Rule of Court 3.1348(a).) The court does not find substantial justification but reduces the amount to reflect that no reply brief was required. Brooks shall pay $1000 to defendant Diamond Wellness Incorporated within 30 days of entry of this order. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(301/CVA).

rulings.Case Title: ELIZABETH BROOKS VS. DIAMOND WELLNESS INCORPORATED ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Deem Requests For Admission, Set One, Admitted, And Request For Sanctions In The Amount Of $1,275.00; Declaration Of Brianda Herrera In Support Thereof
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 21, 2025, Line 8, DEFENDANT DIAMOND WELLNESS INCORPORATED's Motion To Deem Requests For Admission, Set One, Admitted, And Request For Sanctions In The Amount Of $1,275.00. Hearing required. The unopposed motion for a deemed-admitted order against plaintiff Elizabeth Brooks is granted. Defendant Diamond Wellness Inc. served requests for admission on Brooks, who did not verify her responses. An unverified response to a request for admission is equivalent to failing to respond, although objections are preserved. (See Edmon & Karnow, Cal. Prac. Guide Civ. Pro. Before Trial Ch. 8G-5, 8:1364.1, 8:1364.2.) Brooks responded to RFAs 1-7 and did not object that they could not be answered. The matters in those RFAs are deemed admitted but her vagueness objection to RFA 6 is preserved. (See CCP 2033.280(b), (c).) Defendant also seeks sanctions. "It is mandatory that the court impose a monetary sanction . . . on the party or attorney, or both, whose failure to serve a timely response to requests for admission necessitated this motion." (CCP 2033.280(c).) The court awards sanctions but reduces the amount to reflect that no reply brief was required. Brooks shall pay $1000 to defendant Diamond Wellness Incorporated within 30 days of entry of this order. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. rulings.Case Title: GABRIELLA LEON VS. RESTORATION HARDWARE HOLDINGS, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT RESTORATION HARDWARE, INC.'S MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 21, 2025, Line 9, DEFENDANT RESTORATION HARDWARE, INC.'s Motion To Compel Arbitration. (Part 2 of 2). Based on the sex harassment claim, the case is exempt from arbitration. (See Turner v. Tesla, Inc. (N.D. Cal. 2023) 686 F.Supp.3d 917, 925 ["[T]he text of 402(a) makes clear that its invalidation of an arbitration agreement extends to the entirety of the case"]; Doe v. Second Street Corp. (2024) 105 Cal.App.5th 552, 577 ["By its plain language, then, the statute applies to the entire case, not merely to the sexual assault or sexual harassment claims alleged as a part of the case. It is significant, moreover, that the statute does not require that the pendant claims arise out of the sexual assault or sexual harassment dispute; it is enough that the case relates to the sexual assault or sexual harassment claims."]; Liu v. Miniso Depot CA, Inc. (2024) 105 Cal.App.5th 791 [EFAA exempts entire case, not just sexual harassment claim].) Accordingly, the court denies this motion. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. (End of tentative ruling) =(301/CVA).

rulings.Case Title: GABRIELLA LEON VS. RESTORATION HARDWARE HOLDINGS, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Compel Arbitration
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 21, 2025, Line 9, DEFENDANT RESTORATION HARDWARE, INC.'s Motion To Compel Arbitration. (Part 1 of 2). Defendant's motion to compel arbitration and stay is denied. "The petitioner bears the burden of proving the existence of a valid arbitration agreement by the preponderance of the evidence, and a party opposing the petition bears the burden of proving by a preponderance of the evidence any fact necessary to its defense." (Engalla v. Permanente Medical Group, Inc. (1997) 15 Cal.4th 951, 972.) Here, defendants establish the arbitration agreement and plaintiff does not dispute its existence. (See Gamboa v. Northeast Community Clinic (2021) 72 Cal.App.5th 158 [describing three step process to establish an arbitration agreement].) The parties also apparently agree that the Federal Arbitration Act ("FAA") applies. Plaintiff pleads a claim for sexual harassment and therefore the action is not arbitrable under the federal Ending Forced Arbitration of Sexual Assault and Sexual Harassment Act ("EFAA"). In 2022, Congress amended the FAA by passing the EFAA to render arbitration agreements unenforceable in sexual assault and sexual harassment cases that arise or accrue on or after March 2, 2022. (Doe v. Second Street Corp. (2024) 105 Cal.App.5th 552, 559.) The EFAA voids a pre-dispute arbitration agreement "with respect to a case which . . .relates to . . . the sexual harassment dispute." (9 U.S.C. 402(a) (emphasis added).) Here, plaintiff pleads a cognizable claim for sexual harassment. The court liberally construes plaintiff's complaint per CCP 452. In 2019, the California Legislature clarified harassment law and noted "[a] single incident of harassing conduct is sufficient to create a triable issue regarding the existence of a hostile work environment if the harassing conduct has unreasonably interfered with the plaintiff's work performance or created an intimidating, hostile, or offensive work environment." (Government Code section 12923(b); see also Beltran v. Hard Rock Hotel Licensing, Inc. (2023) 97 Cal.App.5th 865, 878-880 [noting less demanding standard and inapplicability of former cases]; CACI 2524 [defining severe or pervasive]; Bailey v. San Francisco District Attorney's Office (2024) 16 Cal.5th 611 [use of single racial epithet could constitute severe conduct to support harassment claim]; Roby v. McKesson (2009) 47 Cal.4th 686, 710-11 [harassment can be based on rude comments, belittling, and other "business" conduct that sends an offensive message].) In this case, plaintiff alleges several specific instances of sexually harassing conduct. (Complaint, par. 12-14.) (Continued to Part 2). =(301/CVA).

rulings.Case Title: TRACY SIMERLEY ET AL VS. GOLDEN GATE HIGHWAY AND TRANSPORTATION DISTRICT ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT GOLDEN GATE BRIDGE HIGHWAY AND TRANSPORTATION DISTRICT'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 21, 2025, Line 5, DEFENDANT GOLDEN GATE BRIDGE HIGHWAY AND TRANSPORTATION DISTRICT's DEMURRER to COMPLAINT. (Part 2 of 2). The cases Plaintiffs rely on predate Dutra and its expansion of the Miles framework for analyzing general maritime law remedies. Moreover, Miles has not been overruled and continues to hold that "there is no recovery for loss of society in a general maritime action for the wrongful death of a Jones Act seaman." (498 U.S. at p. 33.) It would be a disconsonant result to provide a loss of society recovery only for injuries where the seaman is not killed. Plaintiffs' reliance on American Export Lines, Inc. v. Alvez (1980) 446 U.S. 274, is unpersuasive. This opinion, not directly controlling because it relates to harbor workers rather than seamen, heavily relies on the special-solicitude rationale that Plaintiffs urge here. (Id. at pp. 275-276.) The court concludes that its rationale has been undermined by Miles and Dutra and does not apply it to this different context. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. (End of tentative ruling). =(301/CVA).

rulings.Case Title: TRACY SIMERLEY ET AL VS. GOLDEN GATE HIGHWAY AND TRANSPORTATION DISTRICT ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 21, 2025, Line 5, DEFENDANT GOLDEN GATE BRIDGE HIGHWAY AND TRANSPORTATION DISTRICT's DEMURRER to COMPLAINT. (Part 1 of 2). Defendant's demurrer to the fourth cause of action for loss of consortium is sustained without leave to amend. The Jones Act permits a seaman like plaintiff Tracy Simerley to recover for injuries sustained as a result of negligence, while general maritime law (a species of federal common law) allows him to recover for injuries sustained a result of the unseaworthiness of his ship. At issue here is the fourth cause of action, Mr. Simerley's wife Lynette Simerley's derivative claim for loss of consortium. The parties agree that under the Jones Act there is no loss of consortium recovery, since the Jones Act limits recovery to pecuniary damages. (See Miles v. Apex Marine Corp. (1990) 498 U.S. 19, 32 ["There is no recovery for loss of society in a Jones Act wrongful death action."]; see also Opening Br. at p. 3; Opp. Br. at pp. 9-10 [seeking common law remedy only].) The issue is whether she may recover for loss of consortium as a matter of general maritime law on an action for unseaworthiness. The court concludes she may not. The Supreme Court recently spelled out the framework for analysis: General maritime law provides remedies that are consonant with what the Jones Act provides unless there are strong historic antecedents or policy arguments for the remedy. (See Dutra Group v. Batterton (2019) 588 U.S. 358, 371-375. (Dutra was preceded by Miles, which relies on a similar rationale: "It would be inconsistent with our place in the constitutional scheme were we to sanction more expansive remedies in a judicially created cause of action in which liability is without fault than Congress has allowed in cases of death resulting from negligence." (Miles, 498 U.S. at pp. 32-33.)) In other words, if the remedies provided by the two diverge, it should be for good reason. Thus, for example, only because there is a strong historic tradition of awarding punitive damages in maintenance and cure cases does that remedy remain available. (See Atlantic Sounding Co., Inc. v. Townsend (2009) 557 U.S. 404, 407; see also Dutra, supra, 588 U.S. at p. 372 [distinguishing historic tradition of punitive damages in maintenance and cure cases].) Here, Plaintiffs offer no persuasive reason to offer a different remedy as a matter of federal common law from that provided by the Jones Act. They point to both a historic tradition and the policy argument of special solicitude for all plaintiffs involved in general maritime cases, but a lopsided majority of courts reject these contentions. (See Smith v. Trinidad Corp., 992 F.2d 996 ["We agree with the district court that the Supreme Court's holding in Miles has changed the law, and that wives of injured mariners may no longer sue the ship for damages for their nonpecuniary losses, if any, caused by the injuries to the spouse."]; Murray v. Anthony J. Bertucci Constr. Co., Inc., 958 F.2d 127, 132 (5th Cir.), cert. denied, 506 U.S. 865 (1992) ["the spouse of an injured seaman has no cause of action for loss of society under the general maritime law"]; Lollie v. Brown Marine Serv., Inc., 995 F.2d 1565 (11th Cir. 1993) ["we hold that neither the Jones Act nor general maritime law authorizes recovery for loss of society or consortium in personal injury cases"]; Horsley v. Mobil Oil Corp., 15 F.3d 200, 203 (1st Cir. 1994) ["an admiralty court may not extend the remedies available in an unseaworthiness action under the general maritime law to include punitive damages or damages for loss of parental or spousal society."].) (Continued to Part 2) =(301/CVA).
